findings.summary: infrastructure/rendering/__init__.py and infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py use absolute intra-package imports while all other infrastructure subpackage __init__.py files use relative imports
evidence: rendering/__init__.py line 12: `from infrastructure.rendering.config import RenderingConfig` — absolute path into own package
reporting/__init__.py line 11: `from infrastructure.reporting.dashboard_generator import ...` — absolute path into own package
core/__init__.py, validation/__init__.py, publishing/__init__.py all use `from .module import ...` (relative imports) throughout
No comment or docstring explains the deviation in rendering or reporting
findings.suggestion: Convert rendering/__init__.py and reporting/__init__.py to relative imports (`from .config import RenderingConfig`, `from .dashboard_generator import ...`, etc.) to match every other infrastructure subpackage and allow the packages to be relocated or tested in isolation.

findings.summary: Several modules in scientific/ and documentation/ acquire loggers via bare logging.getLogger() instead of the project-standard get_logger() from infrastructure.core.logging_utils
evidence: infrastructure/scientific/validation.py line 16: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` — bypasses get_logger()
infrastructure/scientific/benchmarking.py line 20: same bare pattern
infrastructure/documentation/glossary_gen.py line 17: same bare pattern
infrastructure/publishing/citations.py line 12: same bare pattern
All core/, most validation/, and multi_project.py use `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger; logger = get_logger(__name__)` as the standard
findings.suggestion: Replace bare `logging.getLogger(__name__)` calls with `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger; logger = get_logger(__name__)` in all affected modules to ensure consistent handler and formatter setup across the infrastructure layer.

findings.summary: Several files in documentation/ and one function in validation/integrity.py use os.path while the rest of the codebase exclusively uses pathlib.Path
evidence: generate_glossary_cli.py lines 19, 23, 25, 49, 53-56: exclusive use of os.path.abspath, os.path.join, os.path.exists, os.path.dirname, os.makedirs
glossary_gen.py lines 53, 70: os.path.join and os.path.relpath inside generator function
integrity.py line 724: `os.path.getctime(output_dir)` — single os.path call inside create_integrity_manifest(), surrounded by pathlib usage throughout the same file
All other infrastructure modules (core/, validation/, publishing/, rendering/) use pathlib.Path exclusively
findings.suggestion: Rewrite generate_glossary_cli.py and glossary_gen.py to use pathlib.Path API. Replace the os.path.getctime call in integrity.py with `output_dir.stat().st_ctime`.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/validate_markdown_cli.py manually mutates sys.path at module level; no other infrastructure CLI module does this
evidence: validate_markdown_cli.py line 21: `repo_root = Path(__file__).parent.parent`
validate_markdown_cli.py line 22: `sys.path.insert(0, str(repo_root))`
infrastructure/rendering/cli.py, infrastructure/publishing/cli.py, infrastructure/core/cli.py — none of these perform sys.path mutation
findings.suggestion: Remove the sys.path manipulation and rely on the package being installed via `uv sync`, or invoke via `python -m infrastructure.validation.validate_markdown_cli`. If direct-script invocation is genuinely required, document the reason and apply the approach consistently to all CLI entrypoints.

findings.summary: Within infrastructure/core/file_operations.py, some functions raise FileOperationError on failure while others catch exceptions, log, and return False — callers cannot use a single consistent handling strategy
evidence: clean_output_directory() raises FileOperationError on OSError (lines 42, 57) — caller must use try/except
clean_root_output_directory() catches Exception, logs error, returns False (lines 306-308) — caller must check return value
clean_coverage_files() catches Exception, logs error, returns False (lines 376-378) — caller must check return value
copy_final_deliverables() catches Exception on shutil.copytree, appends to stats['errors'] list, returns early with partial stats dict (lines 453-457) — caller must inspect nested dict key

calculate_file_hash() raises FileOperationError on PermissionError (lines 584-585) but returns None on generic OSError (lines 587-588) — two different surfaces for the same I/O failure class within one function
findings.suggestion: Decide per function: pipeline-critical functions (clean_output_directory) should raise typed exceptions consistently; best-effort utilities (coverage cleanup, log archival) should stay as return-bool but be documented as non-fatal. The current inconsistency is within a single file. For calculate_file_hash, unify to either always raise FileOperationError or always return None, and document the contract in the docstring.

findings.summary: Multiple verify_* functions in infrastructure/validation/integrity.py catch Exception inside loops, set a boolean flag False, and continue — losing per-item error detail and in one case aborting remaining checks silently
evidence: verify_file_integrity() lines 77-79: `except Exception as e: logger.error(...); integrity[str(file_path)] = False` — original exception not stored in report
verify_cross_references() lines 125-126: `except Exception as e: logger.error(...); continue` — no record of which file caused the failure in the returned dict

verify_data_consistency() lines 190-193: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...); consistency['data_integrity'] = False; break` — the `break` silently abandons all remaining files after first failure, so callers receive partial results with no indication they are incomplete
findings.suggestion: In verify_data_consistency, remove the `break` so all files are checked and all failures are surfaced. Across all three functions, collect per-item failure details into the IntegrityReport.issues or .warnings lists rather than only flipping a top-level boolean, giving callers actionable information about which specific files or references failed.

findings.summary: infrastructure/scientific/templates.py contains bare `except:` clauses that catch BaseException (including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit), violating the project's otherwise consistent typed exception handling
evidence: scientific/templates.py line 160: bare `except:` with no exception type — catches BaseException
scientific/templates.py line 178: second bare `except:` — same issue
All other infrastructure modules use `except Exception as e:` or more specific typed exceptions; no other bare except clauses were found in the infrastructure layer
findings.suggestion: Replace both bare `except:` clauses with `except Exception as e:` and log the error. Evaluate whether the swallowed exception should be re-raised or reported as a return value rather than silently ignored.

findings.summary: ProjectLogger and its factory functions are pure forwarding wrappers over stdlib logging.Logger with no added policy
evidence: Lines 94-148: every method (debug, info, warning, error, critical, success, header, progress, stage, substep, operation, timing, resource_usage) only forwards to self._logger or a module-level function with no transformation, validation, or policy
Lines 156-175: get_project_logger() is a one-liner returning ProjectLogger(name, level) — adds zero value over calling ProjectLogger directly
Lines 178-197: setup_project_logging() calls setup_logger then get_project_logger, adding only optional file handler — callers could compose setup_logger and get_logger directly
grep shows 99 infrastructure files call get_logger() directly; only 4 files reference ProjectLogger, confirming the wrapper is unused in practice
findings.suggestion: Remove ProjectLogger, get_project_logger, and setup_project_logging. The direct get_logger() pattern already used across 99 modules is the correct abstraction level. Keep backwards-compat aliases in __init__.py if needed.

findings.summary: Two near-identical performance modules define a monitor_performance symbol with different semantics, and __all__ exports a non-existent symbol
evidence: performance.py line 422: monitor_performance = performance_context (alias for a context manager)
performance_monitor.py line 267: def monitor_performance(...) — a completely different decorator factory with the same name

__init__.py line 103 imports monitor_performance from performance_monitor, silently shadowing the alias from performance.py; line 185 lists 'monitor_performance' in __all__ without disambiguating which one

__init__.py line 189: 'monitor_performance_decorator' is in __all__ but is never defined anywhere in the codebase — any caller doing `from infrastructure.core import monitor_performance_decorator` gets an AttributeError
findings.suggestion: Merge performance.py and performance_monitor.py into a single module. Rename the context manager to performance_context (already done), rename the decorator to monitor_performance_decorator (resolving the collision), and delete the ghost __all__ entry.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py is a re-export hub for four satellite modules, creating a multi-hop import chain for callers who only need get_logger
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 24-35: imports from logging_formatters, logging_helpers, and logging_progress then re-exports all of them in __all__ (lines 697-705)
All 99 infrastructure modules import from logging_utils.py, meaning every module transitively loads the full satellite chain even when only get_logger is needed
__all__ re-exports JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter, EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS — formatter internals exposed through the top-level logging facade
findings.suggestion: Collapse logging_helpers and logging_constants into logging_utils.py. Keep logging_progress separate only if it exports Spinner/StreamingProgress as standalone classes. Remove formatter constants from logging_utils.__all__.

findings.summary: psutil is a hard core dependency yet logging_utils.py and performance.py already implement graceful ImportError fallbacks, creating contradictory assumptions
evidence: pyproject.toml line 43: psutil>=5.9.0 listed as a mandatory core dependency
logging_utils.py lines 639-664: try/except ImportError block with logger.debug('psutil not available') — graceful degradation for an already-guaranteed import
performance.py lines 165-174: _get_memory_usage() wraps psutil in try/except ImportError returning 0.0 as fallback
health_check.py (per glob) imports psutil unconditionally at module level — inconsistent with the fallback pattern in the other two modules
findings.suggestion: Either (a) keep psutil as a hard dep and remove the try/except fallbacks since they are dead code, or (b) move psutil to an optional dependency group and ensure all callers use the try/except guard consistently. Option (a) is simpler given the architecture.

findings.summary: 'monitor_performance_decorator' is listed in infrastructure/core/__init__.__all__ but is never defined anywhere
evidence: Line 189: 'monitor_performance_decorator' in __all__
Codebase-wide grep returns zero definitions of monitor_performance_decorator — only this single __all__ entry
Any caller doing `from infrastructure.core import monitor_performance_decorator` will receive an AttributeError at runtime
findings.suggestion: Remove 'monitor_performance_decorator' from __all__ in infrastructure/core/__init__.py. If the decorator form of monitor_performance is needed, define it explicitly with that name in performance_monitor.py and import it correctly.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor's subprocess execution, exit-code handling, and checkpoint writing are not exercised by any test
evidence: test_pipeline.py visible content only tests PipelineConfig dataclass field defaults and custom values (14 test functions total per grep count)
PipelineExecutor — which runs subprocess stages, reads exit codes, and delegates to CheckpointManager — has no execution-path test coverage visible
PipelineExecutor is the core orchestrator called by scripts/execute_pipeline.py and scripts/execute_multi_project.py, making it the highest-impact untested class in the repo
findings.suggestion: Add tests for PipelineExecutor using a real minimal script (e.g. a Python one-liner via subprocess) in tmp_path. Assert exit codes are captured, PipelineStageResult.success is set correctly, and checkpoint files are written. Use pytest's tmp_path fixture.

findings.summary: validate_publication_readiness in publishing/package.py has multiple conditional branches with zero visible test coverage
evidence: test_publishing_core.py tests only create_publication_package (dict return type and key presence) and calculate_file_hash; validate_publication_readiness is neither imported nor called

validate_publication_readiness (lines 228-302) implements completeness scoring, PDF file checks, citation extraction, and figure-pattern detection — at minimum 5 independent conditional branches untested
The function is exported from infrastructure/publishing/__init__.py line 45 as a public API symbol
findings.suggestion: Add tests covering: (1) markdown with all required sections yields ready=True and score>=80, (2) missing sections lower score below 80 and set ready_for_publication=False, (3) empty pdf_files list triggers the 'No PDF files' missing element branch.

findings.summary: The monitor_performance name collision between performance.py and performance_monitor.py is undetected because no test imports both and verifies they are the correct objects
evidence: test_performance_monitor.py tests CodeProfiler and ProfilingMetrics in complete isolation from performance.py

No test imports from infrastructure.core directly and asserts that monitor_performance resolves to the decorator factory (performance_monitor.py) rather than the context-manager alias (performance.py line 422)
The shadow import in __init__.py line 103 means the wrong implementation could be silently swapped without any test failing
findings.suggestion: Add a cross-module import test: `from infrastructure.core import monitor_performance; assert callable(monitor_performance); result = monitor_performance('test')(lambda: None)()` to pin the decorator-factory semantics.

findings.summary: ProjectLogger carries restating one-liner docstrings on five trivial one-line delegate methods
evidence: Lines 94-112: debug(), info(), warning(), error(), critical() each have a single-sentence docstring ('Log debug message.', 'Log info message.', etc.) on a body that is exactly one self._logger.<level>() call
These docstrings restate the method name verbatim and add zero information — canonical AI-generation filler
The pattern appears five times consecutively, consistent with template-expansion rather than authored intent
findings.suggestion: Remove the docstrings from trivial one-line delegate methods or collapse them to a single note on the class. The method names are self-documenting.

findings.summary: EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, and USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING are defined verbatim in both logging_constants.py and logging_utils.py despite logging_constants.py being created explicitly to eliminate this duplication
evidence: logging_constants.py lines 13-31: defines EMOJIS dict (11 keys), USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING
logging_utils.py lines 210-228: defines an identical EMOJIS dict with the same 11 keys and the same two flag variables independently
logging_constants.py module docstring explicitly states it exists to 'avoid duplicate definitions and circular import issues' — the problem it was meant to solve is still present in logging_utils.py
findings.suggestion: Remove EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING from logging_utils.py (lines 210-228) and import them from logging_constants. Update __all__ to export from the single source.

findings.summary: PerformanceMonitor.start() and record_cache_hit() carry multi-line docstrings with Args/Returns/Example sections for bodies that are 4-6 lines of counter resets
evidence: Lines 80-100: start() has a 14-line docstring (including Args: None, Returns: None, Example showing monitor.start()) for a method that calls time.time() and resets five counters
Lines 132-147: record_cache_hit() has a 13-line docstring with Args, Returns, Example for self.cache_hits += 1

Adjacent record_operation() (line 128-130) has only a one-liner docstring for the same type of counter increment — inconsistent verbosity across adjacent methods signals pasted template blocks rather than authored documentation
findings.suggestion: Trim start() and record_cache_hit() docstrings to a single sentence. The Example sections (showing '>>> if data in cache: monitor.record_cache_hit()') illustrate nothing non-obvious.

findings.summary: verify_output_completeness() uses 'if not dir.exists(): ... elif dir.exists():' three times — the elif branch is logically always True and the pattern is copy-paste generation
evidence: Line 627: 'if not figures_dir.exists(): ... elif figures_dir.exists():' — the elif is only reachable if the directory was created between the two checks, which cannot happen here
Lines 654-657 and 677-680: identical pattern repeated for data_dir and tex_dir

The pdf_dir block (lines 603-623) correctly uses a plain 'if pdf_dir.exists():' — the inconsistency confirms the other three blocks were produced by duplicating a template and prepending a not-exists guard without removing the original positive condition
findings.suggestion: Replace 'elif dir.exists():' with a plain 'else:' or restructure to a single 'if dir.exists():' block. The not-exists branch that sets flags and appends to missing_outputs should be the else clause.

findings.summary: performance.py and performance_monitor.py both live in infrastructure/core/ and cover overlapping concerns (timing, memory, benchmarking), resulting in a name-aliasing workaround and a duplicated benchmark_function
evidence: performance.py line 64: PerformanceMonitor — resource-snapshot class with start/stop pattern
performance_monitor.py line 60: CodeProfiler — cProfile + tracemalloc class with its own monitor() context manager and benchmark_function
performance.py line 421: 'ResourceTracker = PerformanceMonitor' alias added to paper over the name confusion between the two classes
performance_monitor.py line 267: top-level monitor_performance() decorator; performance.py line 422: 'monitor_performance = performance_context' alias — same exported name, different implementations

benchmark_function appears as both a CodeProfiler method (line 139) and a module-level convenience function (line 362) in the same file, with the module-level version creating a new CodeProfiler() on every call
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single performance.py: PerformanceMonitor for lightweight resource snapshots, CodeProfiler for cProfile-based profiling. Remove the ResourceTracker alias, the monitor_performance name collision, and the duplicate benchmark_function.

findings.summary: The logging subsystem is split across five files (logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py) with logging_utils.py acting as a pure re-exporting facade, creating indirection for modules with insufficient mass to justify standalone existence
evidence: logging_constants.py is 31 lines defining only 3 symbols — well below the threshold for a justified standalone module
logging_helpers.py is 88 lines with 2 functions (format_error_with_suggestions, format_duration) that have no reason to live apart from logging_utils.py
logging_utils.py imports all four siblings and re-exports them in its __all__ (lines 667-706) — it contributes no logic of its own that required the fragmentation
The EMOJIS duplication (separate finding) is a direct consequence of the fragmentation creating circular-import pressure that led logging_constants.py to be created as a workaround
findings.suggestion: Consolidate logging_constants.py and logging_helpers.py back into logging_utils.py. Keep logging_formatters.py (JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter are a coherent, testable unit) and logging_progress.py (Spinner, StreamingProgress are distinct UI primitives) as separate files.

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/ is positioned as a peer package to core/, validation/, and rendering/, despite being a narrow PDF-watermarking feature with no importers outside itself
evidence: Grep of 'from infrastructure.steganography' across all .py files finds zero imports from any other infrastructure package — the module is self-contained and unused by the rest of infrastructure
Its domain (PDF watermarking, QR barcodes, hash manifests, PDF encryption) is a publishing/rendering output concern, not a generic infrastructure primitive
Sitting at the same hierarchy level as core/ or validation/ implies equal generality and discoverability weight, which is misleading for navigability
findings.suggestion: Move infrastructure/steganography/ under infrastructure/publishing/ or infrastructure/rendering/ where its PDF-output concern is co-located with related packages. Update all imports (only the steganography package itself and its test suite need updating).

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 files covering 8+ distinct sub-domains (logging, config, pipeline, checkpointing, security, health checks, credentials, multi-project orchestration, progress bars), weakening navigability
evidence: 27 Python files confirmed by Glob in a single package

security.py (SecurityValidator, RateLimiter, SecurityMonitor, SecurityHeaders) has no dependency relationship with logging_formatters.py or progress.py — they share the package only because 'core' acts as a catch-all
health_check.py and credentials.py import from logging_utils but otherwise have no intra-core dependencies — they are stragglers by the import-affinity criterion

pipeline.py, checkpoint.py, multi_project.py, pipeline_summary.py, and script_discovery.py form a coherent pipeline-orchestration domain that would read more clearly as a separate infrastructure/pipeline/ package
findings.suggestion: Extract security.py, health_check.py, credentials.py into infrastructure/security/. Extract pipeline.py, checkpoint.py, multi_project.py, pipeline_summary.py, script_discovery.py into infrastructure/pipeline/. Keep core/ for genuine primitives: logging, config, exceptions, file_operations, retry, performance.

findings.summary: core/__init__.py exports monitor_performance sourced from two different implementations — a context-manager alias in performance.py and a decorator factory in performance_monitor.py — the latter import silently shadows the former
evidence: performance.py line 422: 'monitor_performance = performance_context' (context-manager alias yielding a PerformanceMonitor)
performance_monitor.py line 267: 'def monitor_performance(operation_name, track_memory)' (decorator factory returning a wrapped function)
core/__init__.py line 103: 'from .performance_monitor import monitor_performance' — this import appears after the performance.py import block, silently shadowing the alias
core/__init__.py __all__ lists 'monitor_performance' once at line 185, but its resolved type depends on import order
findings.suggestion: Remove the 'monitor_performance = performance_context' alias from performance.py — performance_context is already a well-named export. Expose only the decorator-factory monitor_performance from performance_monitor.py as the single canonical symbol.

findings.summary: validate_build_artifacts() and verify_output_completeness() in the generic infrastructure validation module hardcode project-specific output filenames (ablation_study.png, convergence_data.npz, recommendation_scalability.png, etc.)
evidence: Lines 459-501: expected_structure dict hardcodes 9 PDF names, 9 TeX names, 5 data filenames, 10 figure filenames specific to code_project outputs

Lines 604-614 and 631-643: verify_output_completeness() independently repeats the same hardcoded lists (ablation_study.png, hyperparameter_sensitivity.png, recommendation_scalability.png, step_size_analysis.png)
These are domain outputs from a specific research project, not generic infrastructure validators

An expected_files parameter exists on validate_build_artifacts() indicating intent for configurability, but the hardcoded defaults silently bake in project-specific assumptions for any caller that omits the argument
findings.suggestion: Remove the hardcoded defaults from both functions. Require callers to supply expected_files explicitly, or define the defaults in projects/code_project/src/ or its config. The infrastructure layer must not know about ablation studies or recommendation scalability figures.

findings.summary: Stage list is copy-pasted across execute_full_pipeline, execute_core_pipeline, and _resume_pipeline rather than composed from a shared definition
evidence: Lines 146-170 (execute_full_pipeline) and 183-204 (execute_core_pipeline) both construct stages lists with identical entries for Clean, Environment Setup, Infrastructure Tests, Project Tests, Analysis, PDF Rendering, Output Validation, and Copy Outputs
_resume_pipeline (lines 340-359) re-builds yet a third variant of the same stage list inline, adding conditional LLM stages
Any addition or rename of a stage requires touching all three sites
findings.suggestion: Extract a single _build_stage_list(include_llm: bool, skip_clean: bool) method that returns the canonical stage list. All three entry points call it, and only the flag arguments vary.

findings.summary: Exit-code-2 (graceful skip) is handled inside _resume_pipeline but not inside _execute_pipeline, splitting the same concern across two call sites
evidence: _resume_pipeline lines 416-424 explicitly checks exit_code == 2 and rebuilds a new PipelineStageResult with success=True
_execute_pipeline (lines 206-243) has no such handling; callers of execute_full_pipeline/execute_core_pipeline do not get the same skip promotion

_run_llm_review/_run_llm_translations call _run_script with allow_skip_code=True, which internally returns True for code 2; but _execute_stage captures only the bool, so exit_code is always stored as 0 or 1 (line 288-293) — the PipelineStageResult.exit_code therefore never equals 2 in the non-resume path, making the resume-path check dead code
findings.suggestion: Centralise skip-code promotion inside _execute_stage: accept an allow_skip_code parameter (passed through from stage spec tuples) and promote there. Remove the redundant post-hoc fixup in _resume_pipeline.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor manually manages root-logger file handlers (add, scan, remove, re-add after clean), which should be an infrastructure logging concern, not a pipeline orchestration concern
evidence: Lines 88-133: _setup_log_file_handler iterates root_logger.handlers, searches by baseFilename, closes and removes old handlers, then appends a new one
Lines 470-486: _run_clean_outputs calls _setup_log_file_handler a second time mid-pipeline to re-attach after the clean stage deletes the directory
Lines 562-598: _flush_log_handlers iterates root_logger.handlers and every named logger's handlers to flush before copy — this is global logger state manipulation inside a pipeline class
findings.suggestion: Move the log-file lifecycle into a LogFileManager or extend setup_logger in logging_utils.py to support rotate-on-recreate. PipelineExecutor calls a single LogFileManager.attach(path) / LogFileManager.reattach() without touching the root logger directly.

findings.summary: performance.py and performance_monitor.py both export a symbol named monitor_performance with incompatible signatures, creating a silent naming collision at the module boundary
evidence: performance.py line 422: monitor_performance = performance_context (a context manager alias)
performance_monitor.py lines 267-289: monitor_performance is a decorator factory taking (operation_name, track_memory)

An importer doing 'from infrastructure.core.performance import monitor_performance' gets the context manager; from performance_monitor gets the decorator — the same exported name means different things at each boundary
findings.suggestion: Rename the alias in performance.py to performance_context only (remove the monitor_performance alias), leaving monitor_performance exclusively as the decorator in performance_monitor.py. Update callers.

findings.summary: render_all in RenderManager appends to outputs only on success but catches sub-renderer exceptions and continues, so callers receive a list that may be shorter than expected with no indication of which renderers failed
evidence: Lines 77-107: Beamer slide failure is caught, logged as warning, and execution continues — nothing is appended to outputs
Lines 110-116: HTML failure is similarly caught and swallowed
Line 118: 'if not outputs: raise TemplateError' only triggers when ALL renderers fail; partial failure is silently returned as a shorter list
Callers have no way to distinguish '1 format succeeded' from '2 formats succeeded' without inspecting file names
findings.suggestion: Return a RenderResult dataclass containing both succeeded paths and per-renderer failure reasons, or at minimum raise TemplateError when any configured renderer fails unless explicitly marked optional.

findings.summary: StagePerformanceTracker stores start_memory and start_io as instance attributes set inside start_stage rather than as method-local or per-stage dataclass, making the object stateful in a fragile way
evidence: Lines 267-281: start_stage sets self.start_memory and self.start_io (never declared in __init__)
end_stage (line 284) accesses self.start_io without guarding for the case where start_stage was never called beyond 'if self.start_time is None: return {}'
If start_stage raises after self.start_time is set but before self.start_io is set, end_stage would raise AttributeError on self.start_io
__init__ (lines 262-265) initialises only self.stages and self.start_time; start_memory and start_io are absent
findings.suggestion: Declare self.start_memory: float = 0.0 and self.start_io: Optional[Any] = None in __init__, or bundle per-stage start data into a local _StageSnapshot dataclass stored as one attribute.

findings.summary: ProfilingMetrics.__post_init__ mutates Optional[int] fields in-place using integer division, silently discarding sub-MB precision and relying on the caller to not re-read the raw byte values
evidence: Lines 37-44: if self.memory_peak: self.memory_peak = self.memory_peak // (1024*1024) converts bytes to MB destructively
The field is typed Optional[int] (bytes semantics before __post_init__, MB semantics after) — the same field name carries two different units depending on lifecycle phase
to_dict() at line 53 labels the key 'memory_peak_mb' suggesting MB, but downstream code reading .memory_peak directly sees MB not bytes — unit ambiguity embedded in the type
findings.suggestion: Store raw bytes in a private _memory_peak_bytes field and expose a @property memory_peak_mb -> Optional[float] that converts on access. The dataclass fields should have stable, single-unit semantics.

findings.summary: verify_cross_references sets all five integrity keys to False when any missing label is found, making the per-type breakdown it returns meaningless
evidence: Lines 96-102: integrity dict is initialised with per-type keys: equations, figures, tables, sections, citations
Lines 129-133: missing_labels check does 'integrity = {k: False for k in integrity}' — all keys go False regardless of which reference type failed
The fine-grained dict structure implies the caller can determine which reference type is broken, but the implementation collapses everything to a uniform False
findings.suggestion: Map reference label prefixes (e.g., 'fig:', 'eq:', 'tab:') to their integrity keys and set only the affected keys to False, or simplify the return to a single bool and remove the misleading per-type dict.

findings.summary: The fallback model loop in LLMClient.query references start_time which is not defined in that scope
evidence: Line 257: generation_time = time_module.time() - start_time inside the except LLMConnectionError fallback loop

start_time is never assigned in the query() method; the only timing variable is response_text, generation_time from _time_call() at line 226-228, which is inside the try block and would not be in scope if the exception was raised before that assignment
This means the fallback path will raise NameError at line 257 when it succeeds on a fallback model
findings.suggestion: Capture start_time = time_module.time() at the top of query() before the try block, consistent with how it is used in query_raw() and other methods.

findings.summary: performance.py and performance_monitor.py serve the same domain (timing and memory measurement) with overlapping classes and no clear ownership boundary
evidence: performance.py exports PerformanceMonitor (resource tracker), PerformanceMetrics, ResourceUsage, StagePerformanceTracker, performance_context
performance_monitor.py exports CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, monitor_performance decorator, benchmark_function, profile_memory_usage
Both modules use psutil for process memory; both wrap tracemalloc; both expose context managers for timing

performance.py module docstring (line 4) explicitly says 'For function-level profiling and timing decorators, see performance_monitor.py' — the boundary is acknowledged but the implementations overlap rather than compose
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into one module or make the split strict: performance.py owns pipeline-stage granularity (StagePerformanceTracker only) and performance_monitor.py owns function/code granularity (CodeProfiler only), with no shared psutil wrappers.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report accepts 9 parameters, 6 of which are Optional, making call sites verbose and the function boundary diffuse
evidence: Lines 277-287: signature is (stage_results, total_duration, repo_root, test_results=None, validation_results=None, performance_metrics=None, error_summary=None, output_statistics=None, project_name=None)
The function body (lines 303-338) handles each optional separately with ad-hoc mutation (lines 318-325 mutate output_statistics in-place to add log_file info)
Callers must pass positional + keyword soup; the large optional set is a signal these fields belong in a config/input dataclass
findings.suggestion: Introduce a ReportInput dataclass (or extend PipelineReport as the input type) so callers construct it with named fields and pass one object. The function signature becomes (input: ReportInput, repo_root: Path) -> PipelineReport.

findings.summary: CheckpointManager resolves the default checkpoint directory by walking up from __file__, coupling the module's filesystem location to runtime path resolution
evidence: Lines 77-79: repo_root = Path(__file__).parent.parent.parent followed by checkpoint_dir = repo_root / 'projects' / project_name / ...
This assumes checkpoint.py is always exactly 3 directory levels below the repo root; moving the file or running from an installed package breaks the path silently
PipelineExecutor already holds config.repo_root (pipeline.py line 76); it passes only project_name to CheckpointManager, forcing CheckpointManager to re-derive repo_root itself
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit repo_root: Optional[Path] = None parameter to CheckpointManager.__init__ and pass config.repo_root from PipelineExecutor. Remove the __file__-relative derivation.

findings.summary: performance_context yields the monitor to the caller but also calls monitor.stop() in the finally block, so any caller that calls monitor.stop() explicitly (as shown in the docstring example) will get a RuntimeError on the second call
evidence: Lines 210-221: performance_context yields monitor; finally block calls monitor.stop() unconditionally

Docstring example at lines 203-208 shows '... metrics = monitor.stop()' inside the with block — this is the documented usage, but it will cause stop() to be called twice: once by the caller, once by finally

PerformanceMonitor.stop() at line 108 raises RuntimeError('Monitor not started') if start_time is None; after first stop(), start_time is not reset so the second stop() would also fail with the same error
findings.suggestion: Either yield the monitor without calling stop() in finally (let the caller decide), or do not yield the monitor at all and capture metrics internally. The current API promises both managed lifetime and caller control, which conflict.
